findings.title: eval() executes arbitrary attacker-controlled input
findings.evidence: renderExpression now returns `eval(input)` where `input: string` is an untrusted function parameter (previously `input.trim()`). In a billing-worker context this is a remote code-execution / code-injection vector — arbitrary JavaScript in `input` executes with the worker's privileges.

notes: The raw diff exactly matches the report: the only change is `input.trim()` -> `eval(input)` in src/renderExpression.ts. Diff Drift's single high-severity flag is accurate and corroborated by the diff.
Scope is small and unambiguous (one file, one line), so confidence is high. No safe-listing, sandbox, or input validation is present around the eval call.
Function still annotated to return `string`, but `eval` can return any type — a secondary type-safety concern, minor relative to the RCE risk.

evaluator.note: Benchmark v4 blind run against the engine-v2 binary; packet-only context.
